subtopic_detail: Croup severity-graded dexamethasone dosing
stem:
An 18-month-old boy presents to the emergency department at 02:00 with a barking cough that began two days ago following coryza. Overnight he woke with noisy breathing audible from his cot. He has had no fevers above 38.0 degrees Celsius and is taking sips of water.

On arrival you hear inspiratory stridor at rest while he sits calmly on his mother's lap, with mild intercostal recession. He speaks in short sentences and is not drooling. Vital signs are within normal limits for age. There is no urticaria, no preceding choking episode, and immunisations are up to date.

data_table.Vital signs: within normal limits for age
data_table.Examination: inspiratory stridor at rest, mild intercostal recession, calm and well perfused, no drooling, oropharynx unremarkable
data_table.Investigations: clinical diagnosis, no imaging or bloods performed

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate corticosteroid management?

options.text: Oral dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg as a single dose now
options.rationale: Stridor at rest with mild recession but no agitation places this child in the moderate Westley band. RCH directs a single oral dose of 0.15 mg/kg for both mild and moderate croup; onset is within 30 minutes and a second dose is rarely needed. (Source: RCH CPG: Croup)

options.text: Oral dexamethasone 0.6 mg/kg as a single dose now
options.rationale: The 0.6 mg/kg dose is reserved by RCH for severe croup (stridor at rest with marked recession, agitation, exhaustion, or hypoxia). Using it here would expose the child to four times the steroid load without added benefit. (Source: RCH CPG: Croup)

options.text: Nebulised adrenaline 5 mL of 1 in 1000 with cardiac monitoring
options.rationale: Nebulised adrenaline is indicated for severe croup or impending obstruction, not moderate disease. Its benefit is short lived (30 to 90 minutes) and mandates 3-hour observation for rebound stridor. (Source: RCH CPG: Croup)

options.text: Inhaled salbutamol 6 puffs via spacer with face mask now
options.rationale: Beta-2 agonists target lower-airway smooth muscle and have no role in subglottic oedema. Reaching for salbutamol misclassifies stridor as wheeze. (Source: RCH CPG: Croup; Therapeutic Guidelines: Respiratory)

options.text: Discharge home with paracetamol and humidified air advice
options.rationale: Even mild croup warrants a single dose of dexamethasone in current RCH guidance because it shortens illness and prevents return visits. Humidification has no evidence base. (Source: RCH CPG: Croup)

explanation.summary: Croup severity in Australia is stratified using the Westley framework: mild (no stridor at rest), moderate (stridor at rest with mild to moderate recession but calm), and severe (marked recession, agitation, or exhaustion). RCH gives a single oral dose of dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg for both mild and moderate croup. The higher 0.6 mg/kg dose is reserved for severe disease. Nebulised adrenaline is layered on top for severe croup and obligates a 3-hour observation window for rebound.
key_points: Stridor at rest plus calm child plus mild recession equals moderate croup
Mild and moderate croup share the same dexamethasone dose (0.15 mg/kg PO)
0.6 mg/kg is reserved for severe disease and adds no benefit at lower severities
Nebulised adrenaline requires 3 hours of observation for rebound
explanation.pearls: Oral dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg acts within 30 minutes; a second dose is rarely indicated. Reserve 0.6 mg/kg for severe disease and mandate 3-hour observation after any nebulised adrenaline.

subtopic_detail: First febrile UTI imaging cascade in toddlers (RCH)
stem:
A 14-month-old girl presents with two days of fever to 39.2 degrees Celsius and reduced feeding. There is no rash, no respiratory symptoms, and no clear focus on examination. A clean-catch midstream urine sample collected with a maternal-assist technique grows greater than 10^8 colony-forming-units per litre of pure Escherichia coli, sensitive to trimethoprim and cefalexin.

She defervesces within 36 hours of starting oral trimethoprim and is feeding normally by review at 48 hours. Her antenatal scans were normal, she is fully immunised, and her growth tracks the 50th centile. There is no history of constipation, no abdominal mass, and no family history of vesicoureteric reflux.

data_table.Vital signs: afebrile at review, otherwise within normal limits
data_table.Examination: well perfused, no abdominal mass, normal external genitalia, no spinal anomaly
data_table.Investigations: MSU pure growth >10^8 CFU/L E. coli (sensitive to trimethoprim and cefalexin), renal function within normal limits

lead_in: What is the most appropriate imaging recommendation?

options.text: Renal tract ultrasound during the acute illness, before discharge
options.rationale: RCH reserves acute-phase ultrasound for atypical UTI features such as poor response to 48 hours of antibiotics, non-E. coli organism, sepsis, abdominal mass, or rising creatinine. None of these apply. (Source: RCH CPG: Urinary tract infection)

options.text: No routine imaging; arrange clinical review and treat any recurrence promptly
options.rationale: This is a typical first UTI in a child over 6 months with normal antenatal imaging and prompt response. RCH does not recommend routine renal ultrasound, DMSA, or MCUG after a single typical UTI in this age group. (Source: RCH CPG: Urinary tract infection)

options.text: DMSA scan at 4 to 6 months to assess for renal cortical scarring
options.rationale: DMSA scanning sits in NICE pathways but is not part of the RCH cascade for a single typical UTI. Routine scarring screens add radiation without changing management here. (Source: RCH CPG: Urinary tract infection)

options.text: Micturating cystourethrogram to exclude vesicoureteric reflux
options.rationale: MCUG is reserved for recurrent UTI, abnormal ultrasound findings, or atypical infection within the RCH cascade. It is not indicated after a single typical infection. (Source: RCH CPG: Urinary tract infection)

options.text: Outpatient renal ultrasound within 6 weeks of completing antibiotics
options.rationale: Routine delayed ultrasound after a single typical UTI is a NICE construct that RCH does not adopt for children with normal antenatal imaging. It generates incidental findings with no management change. (Source: RCH CPG: Urinary tract infection)

explanation.summary: The RCH urinary tract infection guideline diverges from NICE on imaging after a first UTI. For a child over 6 months with a typical first UTI (E. coli, prompt response, no atypical features), no routine imaging is recommended provided antenatal scans were normal. Ultrasound during illness is reserved for atypical features. DMSA and MCUG are not part of the RCH cascade and are deployed only after recurrent UTI or abnormal ultrasound.
key_points: Typical first UTI in a child over 6 months requires no routine imaging at RCH
Atypical features that trigger acute ultrasound: non-E. coli, no response by 48 h, sepsis, abdominal mass, rising creatinine
DMSA and MCUG are NICE constructs not in the RCH cascade for a single typical UTI
Antenatal renal imaging history changes the threshold for any further imaging
explanation.pearls: RCH does not perform routine post-UTI ultrasound after the first typical infection over 6 months; reserve imaging for atypical features or recurrence within 6 months.

subtopic_detail: Bronchiolitis low-flow oxygen first-line per RCH
stem:
A 4-month-old infant is admitted with three days of coryza followed by worsening wheeze and feeding difficulty. He has taken approximately 40 percent of his usual milk volume over the preceding 24 hours.

On arrival to the ward he has pulse 160, blood pressure 86 by 50 mmHg, respiratory rate 56, SpO2 88 percent in room air, temperature 37.6 degrees Celsius. He has moderate subcostal recession and audible widespread wheeze with bilateral inspiratory crackles. He is alert and consolable in his mother's arms. There has been no apnoea reported by parents or nursing staff, immunisations are up to date, and there is no relevant past medical history.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 160, BP 86/50, RR 56, SpO2 88% room air, temperature 37.6 degrees Celsius
data_table.Examination: moderate subcostal recession, widespread wheeze with bilateral crackles, alert, well perfused
data_table.Investigations: clinical diagnosis of bronchiolitis, no chest X-ray performed

lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial respiratory support?

options.text: Heated humidified high-flow nasal cannula at 2 L/kg/min from the outset
options.rationale: PREDICT and RCH reserve HFNC for failure of low-flow oxygen after a defined trial (usually 1 to 2 hours). Initial escalation to HFNC has not been shown to reduce ICU transfer compared with starting low-flow. (Source: PREDICT Australasian Bronchiolitis Guideline; RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Nebulised salbutamol 6 puffs via spacer plus oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg
options.rationale: Bronchiolitis in under-12-month-olds does not respond to beta-2 agonists or systemic corticosteroids; both are explicitly discouraged by the PREDICT guideline. (Source: PREDICT Australasian Bronchiolitis Guideline)

options.text: Low-flow nasal prong oxygen at 1 to 2 L/min titrated to SpO2 at least 90 percent
options.rationale: RCH and PREDICT both start with low-flow nasal prong oxygen at 1 to 2 L/min targeting SpO2 of at least 90 percent. HFNC is reserved for failure of this approach after 1 to 2 hours. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis; PREDICT Australasian Bronchiolitis Guideline)

options.text: Nebulised hypertonic saline 3 percent with oxygen via face mask
options.rationale: Nebulised hypertonic saline does not reduce admission length or ICU transfer in Australian trials and is not part of standard RCH or PREDICT first-line therapy. (Source: PREDICT Australasian Bronchiolitis Guideline)

options.text: Continuous positive airway pressure via face mask in a monitored bed
options.rationale: Mask CPAP is escalation therapy after low-flow and HFNC failure. Skipping to it bypasses the stepped pathway and is not appropriate first-line in an alert, consolable infant. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

explanation.summary: Australian bronchiolitis care (PREDICT 2024 + RCH) is supportive: fluids and oxygen, with a deliberately stepped approach to respiratory support. Low-flow nasal prong oxygen at 1 to 2 L/min targeting SpO2 of at least 90 percent is first line; HFNC is reserved for failure of low-flow after 1 to 2 hours. Bronchodilators, corticosteroids, nebulised saline, and antibiotics have no role unless an alternative diagnosis emerges.
key_points: Bronchiolitis SpO2 target is at least 90 percent (not 92 to 94)
Start low-flow nasal prong oxygen before HFNC
Salbutamol, steroids, and hypertonic saline are not first-line
HFNC is rescue therapy when low-flow fails after a 1 to 2 hour trial
explanation.pearls: Start low-flow nasal prong oxygen at 1 to 2 L/min targeting SpO2 90 percent; escalate to HFNC only after 1 to 2 hours of inadequate response.

subtopic_detail: Henoch-Schonlein purpura with renal warning features
stem:
A 7-year-old girl presents to the paediatric emergency department with a one-week history of palpable purpura over the buttocks and shins, intermittent crampy abdominal pain, and bilateral ankle swelling. Her parents report her urine has been a darker colour than usual for the past 48 hours. She had a viral upper respiratory infection 10 days earlier and is otherwise well.

On examination she is alert with pulse 96, blood pressure 132 by 88 mmHg, respiratory rate 18, SpO2 100 percent on room air, temperature 36.9 degrees Celsius. There is no rash above the waist and joints are not actively inflamed. Urinalysis shows protein 3+ and blood 3+. Serum creatinine is 92 micromol/L (35-65), albumin 28 g/L (35-50), and renal tract ultrasound is structurally normal.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 96, BP 132/88, RR 18, SpO2 100% room air, temperature 36.9 degrees Celsius
data_table.Examination: palpable purpura buttocks and shins, bilateral ankle oedema, abdomen soft, no peritonism
data_table.Investigations: urinalysis protein 3+ blood 3+; creatinine 92 micromol/L (35-65); albumin 28 g/L (35-50); renal ultrasound structurally normal

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Symptomatic care with simple analgesia and primary-care review in one week
options.rationale: Although most HSP is self-limiting, this child shows three features that mandate escalation: hypertension above the 95th centile, nephrotic-range proteinuria, and an elevated creatinine. Routine community follow-up alone would miss progression to crescentic glomerulonephritis. (Source: RCH CPG: Henoch-Schonlein purpura)

options.text: Oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg for two weeks with outpatient paediatric review
options.rationale: Steroids may reduce abdominal pain in HSP but they do not prevent or treat established nephritis. Starting steroids before specialist assessment risks anchoring on the wrong therapy while serious renal disease evolves. (Source: KHA-CARI Guidelines; RCH CPG: Henoch-Schonlein purpura)

options.text: Commence amoxicillin pending blood cultures and admit for observation
options.rationale: There is no infective source and the platelet count is normal, making sepsis or meningococcaemia unlikely. Empirical antibiotics here would not address the renal trajectory and add no benefit. (Source: RCH CPG: Henoch-Schonlein purpura)

options.text: Admit for blood-pressure management and urgent paediatric nephrology referral for biopsy
options.rationale: Hypertension, heavy proteinuria, and rising creatinine in HSP define warning features that prompt admission, blood-pressure control, and urgent nephrology referral for likely renal biopsy and immunosuppression planning. (Source: RCH CPG: Henoch-Schonlein purpura; KHA-CARI Guidelines)

options.text: Send antinuclear antibody and ANCA panel and review when results return
options.rationale: Serology rarely reframes a textbook HSP presentation and there is no diagnostic uncertainty here that would justify deferring management for results. Time-to-biopsy is the live decision. (Source: KHA-CARI Guidelines)

explanation.summary: Henoch-Schonlein purpura (IgA vasculitis) in children is usually self-limiting but renal involvement occurs in around 30 percent and drives long-term morbidity. Warning features that change management include hypertension above the 95th centile, nephrotic-range proteinuria (more than 2+ on dipstick or PCR >250 mg/mmol), and elevated creatinine. The presence of any of these triggers admission, blood-pressure control, and urgent paediatric nephrology referral with biopsy to grade nephritis and guide immunosuppression. Steroids treat abdominal and joint symptoms but do not prevent or treat nephritis.
key_points: HSP nephritis warning triad: hypertension, nephrotic-range proteinuria, raised creatinine
Most HSP is self-limiting; warning features change management entirely
Steroids do not prevent or treat nephritis - biopsy guides immunosuppression
Hypertension above the 95th centile for age and height needs immediate attention
explanation.pearls: HSP with proteinuria above 2+, hypertension above the 95th centile, or rising creatinine warrants paediatric nephrology referral within 24 hours for biopsy planning.

subtopic_detail: Pre-transfer benzylpenicillin in suspected meningococcal sepsis
stem:
A 2-year-old boy is brought to a rural emergency department by his parents after waking unwell with fever, irritability, and a rash that began 90 minutes before arrival. The parents describe him as 'floppy' and unwilling to be put down.

On examination he is mottled, with pulse 180, blood pressure 72 by 40 mmHg, respiratory rate 44, SpO2 95 percent on room air, temperature 39.4 degrees Celsius. Capillary refill at the sternum is 5 seconds. There are non-blanching purple spots over the lower limbs and trunk, expanding under observation. He does not have neck stiffness and is rousable but drowsy. Intravenous access has failed twice. The nearest paediatric retrieval team estimates 75 minutes to arrival.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 180, BP 72/40, RR 44, SpO2 95% room air, temperature 39.4 degrees Celsius
data_table.Examination: mottled, CRT 5 sec, non-blanching purpuric rash expanding under observation, drowsy but rousable
data_table.Investigations: no peripheral IV access after two attempts; venous lactate from initial draw 6.2 mmol/L (<2.0)

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next action while preparing intraosseous access?

options.text: Withhold antibiotics until blood cultures can be obtained via the IO line
options.rationale: Delaying antibiotics for cultures in suspected meningococcal disease worsens mortality. Cultures are taken at the same time as IO access but never gate the first antibiotic dose. (Source: CDNA Invasive Meningococcal Disease Guideline)

options.text: Give intravenous dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg before any antibiotic dose
options.rationale: Adjunctive dexamethasone in paediatric meningitis is given with or just before the first antibiotic dose, not as a standalone first move, and the antibiotic remains the time-critical intervention. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Give intramuscular benzylpenicillin 60 mg/kg into the lateral thigh now
options.rationale: When IV access is delayed in suspected meningococcal sepsis, the CDNA guideline and Therapeutic Guidelines explicitly endorse intramuscular benzylpenicillin immediately while access is being secured. Pre-transfer antibiotics reduce mortality. (Source: CDNA Invasive Meningococcal Disease Guideline; Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Defer all antibiotics until paediatric retrieval team arrival in 75 minutes
options.rationale: Each hour of antibiotic delay in invasive meningococcal disease increases mortality. CDNA mandates antibiotic administration at the point of suspicion, never withheld for retrieval. (Source: CDNA Invasive Meningococcal Disease Guideline)

options.text: Attempt a third peripheral cannula for fluid resuscitation before any antibiotic
options.rationale: Repeated peripheral attempts after two failures waste time. The RCH paediatric sepsis pathway calls for intraosseous access after failed IV and intramuscular antibiotics if even IO is delayed. (Source: RCH CPG: Sepsis)

explanation.summary: Pre-transfer antibiotics for suspected meningococcal disease save lives. CDNA and Therapeutic Guidelines both endorse intramuscular benzylpenicillin (50 to 60 mg/kg to a maximum of 1.2 g) into the lateral thigh when IV access is delayed. Cultures and adjunctive corticosteroids do not gate the first dose. Intraosseous access remains the fastest reliable route for fluids and ongoing antibiotic infusions when IV attempts fail, but should not delay an IM dose if IO insertion itself is taking time.
key_points: IM benzylpenicillin 60 mg/kg is endorsed when IV/IO access is delayed
Cultures and dexamethasone never gate the first antibiotic dose
RCH paediatric sepsis pathway: IV/IO access then cultures then antibiotic within the hour
Each hour of antibiotic delay measurably increases meningococcal mortality
explanation.pearls: When IV access is delayed in suspected meningococcal sepsis, give IM benzylpenicillin 60 mg/kg (max 1.2 g) into the lateral thigh while IO insertion proceeds in parallel.

subtopic_detail: Fluid resuscitation in paediatric DKA with shock
stem:
A 9-year-old girl with no past medical history is brought in by ambulance with two weeks of polyuria and polydipsia followed by 24 hours of vomiting and abdominal pain. The paramedics report she has become progressively more drowsy in the past hour.

In the resuscitation bay she has pulse 144, blood pressure 86 by 48 mmHg, respiratory rate 38 with Kussmaul pattern, SpO2 98 percent on room air, temperature 36.7 degrees Celsius. Capillary refill at the sternum is 4 seconds and peripheries are cool. GCS is 13 (E3 V4 M6). Bedside blood glucose is 32 mmol/L (3.5-7.7), beta-hydroxybutyrate is 5.8 mmol/L (<0.6), venous pH 7.05 and bicarbonate 6 mmol/L (22-32). She weighs 28 kg.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 144, BP 86/48, RR 38 with Kussmaul breathing, SpO2 98% room air, temperature 36.7 degrees Celsius
data_table.Examination: drowsy GCS 13, cool peripheries, CRT 4 sec at sternum, dry mucous membranes
data_table.Investigations: BSL 32 mmol/L (3.5-7.7), beta-hydroxybutyrate 5.8 mmol/L (<0.6), pH 7.05, bicarbonate 6 mmol/L (22-32), Na 132, K 5.1, weight 28 kg

lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial fluid bolus?

options.text: Insulin infusion 0.1 units/kg/h commenced immediately with no fluid bolus
options.rationale: Insulin in DKA must follow at least one fluid bolus, and is typically delayed 1 to 2 hours after fluid restoration begins. Starting insulin before fluid resuscitation precipitates cardiovascular collapse and worsens cerebral oedema risk. (Source: RCH CPG: Diabetic Ketoacidosis)

options.text: 20 mL/kg of 0.9 percent sodium chloride over 5 to 10 minutes, then reassess
options.rationale: Standard septic-shock bolus volumes in DKA are linked to cerebral oedema and worsened neurological outcomes. RCH and ISPAD explicitly halve the bolus in this context. (Source: RCH CPG: Diabetic Ketoacidosis; ISPAD 2022)

options.text: 20 mL/kg of compound sodium lactate over 5 to 10 minutes, then reassess
options.rationale: Hartmann's solution is acceptable as maintenance fluid in DKA but the bolus rate is the live problem here. A 20 mL/kg rapid bolus carries the same cerebral-oedema risk regardless of crystalloid choice. (Source: ISPAD Clinical Practice Consensus Guidelines 2022)

options.text: 10 mL/kg of 0.9 percent sodium chloride over 30 minutes, then reassess
options.rationale: RCH and ISPAD specify cautious 10 mL/kg boluses over 30 to 60 minutes in DKA with circulatory compromise, to limit the cerebral-oedema risk that rapid fluid shifts trigger. Reassess after each bolus rather than running standard sepsis volumes. (Source: RCH CPG: Diabetic Ketoacidosis; ISPAD 2022)

options.text: Sodium bicarbonate 1 mmol/kg over 60 minutes to correct severe acidosis
options.rationale: Bicarbonate is contraindicated in routine paediatric DKA because it worsens cerebral oedema, paradoxical CNS acidosis, and hypokalaemia. It is not part of any current RCH or ISPAD pathway. (Source: ISPAD Clinical Practice Consensus Guidelines 2022)

explanation.summary: Paediatric DKA with circulatory compromise sits in a deliberately separate fluid-resuscitation pathway. RCH and ISPAD specify 10 mL/kg boluses of 0.9 percent saline over 30 to 60 minutes, with reassessment after each bolus, instead of the 20 mL/kg over 5 to 10 minutes used in septic shock. The reason is cerebral oedema, the leading cause of paediatric DKA mortality, which is precipitated by rapid osmotic shifts. Insulin is delayed at least 1 hour after fluid commencement and bicarbonate is contraindicated.
key_points: DKA shock bolus: 10 mL/kg over 30 to 60 minutes (NOT 20 mL/kg over 5 to 10 min)
Cerebral oedema is the leading cause of paediatric DKA mortality
Insulin starts at least 1 hour after fluid resuscitation begins
Bicarbonate is contraindicated in routine paediatric DKA
explanation.pearls: In paediatric DKA with shock, give 10 mL/kg of saline over 30 to 60 minutes (not the standard 20 mL/kg over 5 to 10 minutes used in septic shock) to limit cerebral-oedema risk.

subtopic_detail: Classical galactosaemia in the septic neonate
stem:
A 9-day-old infant born at term to consanguineous parents is admitted with 24 hours of poor feeding, lethargy, and progressive jaundice. Antenatal screening was unremarkable and the newborn screening sample was sent on day 3 but results are not yet back.

On examination he is jaundiced, hypotonic, with pulse 180, respiratory rate 60, SpO2 95 percent on room air, temperature 38.1 degrees Celsius. The liver is palpable 4 cm below the costal margin. Red-reflex examination at the cot reveals reduced reflex bilaterally with a grey haze.

Investigations: conjugated bilirubin 80 micromol/L (<5), ALT 220 U/L (<40), INR 2.1 (0.8-1.2), bedside glucose 1.8 mmol/L (3.0-6.0), blood culture preliminary growth of Gram-negative rods (later confirmed Escherichia coli), urine reducing substances strongly positive.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 180, RR 60, SpO2 95% room air, temperature 38.1 degrees Celsius
data_table.Examination: jaundiced, hypotonic, hepatomegaly 4 cm, bilateral reduced red reflex with grey haze
data_table.Investigations: conjugated bilirubin 80 micromol/L (<5), ALT 220 U/L (<40), INR 2.1 (0.8-1.2), glucose 1.8 mmol/L (3.0-6.0), blood culture Gram-negative rods (E. coli), urine reducing substances positive

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate combination of actions?

options.text: IV cefotaxime and benzylpenicillin, continue expressed breast milk feeds
options.rationale: Although the antibiotic regimen is correct for neonatal sepsis, continuing lactose-containing feeds will perpetuate galactose-1-phosphate accumulation, deepen the encephalopathy, and worsen the liver injury. The feeds must change at the same time. (Source: RCH CPG: Inherited metabolic disorders)

options.text: IV cefotaxime plus amoxicillin and switch to hydrolysed-protein cow's milk formula
options.rationale: Hydrolysed cow's milk formula still contains lactose and does not solve the galactose problem. Amoxicillin alone does not cover all relevant Gram-negative sepsis pathogens; benzylpenicillin plus cefotaxime is the standard. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic; RCH CPG: Inherited metabolic disorders)

options.text: Continue routine feeds and await newborn screening results before changing formula
options.rationale: Newborn screening reports may take days to weeks; in classical galactosaemia, ongoing galactose exposure causes irreversible cataracts, liver failure, and brain injury during that window. Empirical feed change is mandatory once the diagnosis is suspected. (Source: RCH CPG: Inherited metabolic disorders)

options.text: Phototherapy and IV cefotaxime, defer feed change pending GALT enzyme result
options.rationale: Phototherapy addresses unconjugated hyperbilirubinaemia, not the conjugated picture seen here, and does not address galactose exposure. Awaiting GALT enzyme assay before changing feeds delays the only modifiable cause of ongoing injury. (Source: RCH CPG: Inherited metabolic disorders)

options.text: Stop feeds, IV dextrose-saline plus cefotaxime and benzylpenicillin, switch to soy formula
options.rationale: Suspected classical galactosaemia (E. coli sepsis, conjugated hyperbilirubinaemia, hypoglycaemia, cataracts, positive urinary reducing substances) demands immediate removal of lactose and galactose while sepsis is treated and metabolic confirmation is sought. (Source: RCH CPG: Inherited metabolic disorders; Australasian Society for Inborn Errors of Metabolism)
source_refs: Australasian Society for Inborn Errors of Metabolism

explanation.summary: Classical galactosaemia (GALT deficiency) presents in the first two weeks of life with E. coli sepsis (a near-pathognomonic association), conjugated hyperbilirubinaemia, hepatomegaly, coagulopathy, hypoglycaemia, and bilateral cataracts. Urinary reducing substances are positive while the glucose oxidase strip is negative. Treatment is dual: aggressive treatment of sepsis with standard neonatal cover (IV cefotaxime plus benzylpenicillin) AND immediate removal of dietary galactose by switching to a soy-based formula while definitive GALT enzyme assay is processed. Delay in either limb causes irreversible injury.
key_points: Galactosaemia neonate presents with E. coli sepsis, jaundice, hepatomegaly, hypoglycaemia, cataracts
Urine reducing substances positive while glucose oxidase strip negative is the bedside clue
Treat sepsis AND remove dietary galactose simultaneously - both are time-critical
Soy-based formula is the Australian standard substitute
explanation.pearls: Suspect classical galactosaemia in any neonate with E. coli sepsis plus jaundice plus hypoglycaemia plus positive urinary reducing substances; switch immediately to soy-based formula while awaiting GALT enzyme assay.

sources.label: Australasian Society for Inborn Errors of Metabolism

subtopic_detail: VBAC counselling after one prior LSCS
stem:
A 32-year-old woman in her second pregnancy attends a 36-week midwife-led antenatal review. Her first child was delivered by emergency caesarean three years ago at 39 weeks gestation for a non-reassuring CTG in early labour. There were no complications during recovery, and she has had no other surgical procedures. This pregnancy has been uncomplicated.

She weighs 76 kg with a booking BMI of 26. The current pregnancy is singleton, cephalic, with an estimated foetal weight at the 50th centile. Placental position is fundal-posterior on the 32-week scan. Her midwife refers her to the obstetric clinic to discuss mode of birth. She tells you she is keen to attempt a vaginal birth this time but is worried about uterine rupture.

data_table.Vital signs: within normal limits
data_table.Examination: fundal height 36 cm, cephalic, longitudinal lie, foetal heart 140 bpm
data_table.Investigations: 32-week ultrasound: singleton cephalic, EFW at 50th centile, fundal-posterior placenta

lead_in: What is the most accurate counselling regarding planned vaginal birth after caesarean?

options.text: Vaginal birth success approximately 30 percent; uterine rupture risk approximately 5 percent
options.rationale: Both figures overstate risk and understate benefit by roughly an order of magnitude, which would unfairly bias the conversation against TOLAC. (Source: RANZCOG: Birth after previous caesarean)

options.text: Vaginal birth success approximately 60 percent; uterine rupture risk approximately 2 percent
options.rationale: The success rate is in the right region but the rupture rate is quadruple the cited RANZCOG figure for a single previous low transverse caesarean. (Source: RANZCOG: Birth after previous caesarean)

options.text: Vaginal birth success approximately 70 percent; uterine rupture risk approximately 0.5 percent
options.rationale: RANZCOG quotes 60 to 80 percent success for planned TOLAC after one prior low transverse caesarean, with uterine rupture in approximately 1 in 200. These are the figures to share in counselling. (Source: RANZCOG: Birth after previous caesarean)

options.text: Vaginal birth success approximately 90 percent; uterine rupture risk approximately 0.1 percent
options.rationale: This overstates the likelihood of vaginal delivery and understates rupture risk; quoting these figures would breach informed consent expectations. (Source: RANZCOG: Birth after previous caesarean)

options.text: Vaginal birth contraindicated; recommend elective repeat caesarean at 39 weeks gestation
options.rationale: A single previous low transverse caesarean is not a contraindication to TOLAC and RANZCOG explicitly recommends offering both options after balanced counselling. (Source: RANZCOG: Birth after previous caesarean)

explanation.summary: RANZCOG supports planned vaginal birth after one prior low transverse caesarean (TOLAC) provided there are no contraindications such as classical incision, two or more prior caesareans, placenta praevia, or prior uterine rupture. Counselling must quote a success rate of 60 to 80 percent (approximately 70 percent for single prior LSCS, no other risk factors) and uterine rupture risk of approximately 1 in 200 (0.5 percent). Continuous CTG monitoring is recommended in labour. Induction with prostaglandins increases rupture risk and is generally avoided.
key_points: TOLAC success rate after one LSCS: 60 to 80 percent (RANZCOG)
Uterine rupture risk approximately 1 in 200 (0.5 percent)
Continuous CTG monitoring in labour
Avoid prostaglandin induction; oxytocin augmentation with caution
explanation.pearls: After one prior low transverse caesarean, quote 70 percent success and 0.5 percent uterine rupture risk for planned TOLAC; rupture risk rises with prostaglandin induction.

sources.label: NSW Health: VBAC Clinical Practice Guideline

subtopic_detail: GDM diagnosis on 75g OGTT (ADIPS criteria)
stem:
A 29-year-old woman in her first pregnancy attends for routine antenatal screening at 27 weeks gestation. She has no personal or family history of diabetes. Booking BMI was 24 and she has gained 9 kg in pregnancy so far. The pregnancy is otherwise uncomplicated, with normal morphology scan and a singleton cephalic foetus tracking the 55th centile for estimated weight.

She completes a 75 g oral glucose tolerance test as outpatient. Results: fasting venous plasma glucose 5.3 mmol/L, 1-hour 9.4 mmol/L, 2-hour 7.6 mmol/L. She is asymptomatic. Vital signs are within normal limits. Urine dipstick is negative for protein, glucose, and ketones.

data_table.Vital signs: within normal limits
data_table.Examination: uterus appropriate for dates, foetal heart 145 bpm, no peripheral oedema
data_table.Investigations: 75g OGTT: fasting 5.3 mmol/L, 1-h 9.4 mmol/L, 2-h 7.6 mmol/L; urinalysis negative

lead_in: What is the most appropriate interpretation of these results?

options.text: Normal pregnancy; repeat OGTT at 32 weeks if clinical concerns arise
options.rationale: The fasting value exceeds the ADIPS-IADPSG cut-off, which itself diagnoses GDM. Calling this normal would miss the diagnosis. (Source: ADIPS Consensus Guidelines for GDM)

options.text: Impaired glucose tolerance; arrange repeat 75 g OGTT in 6 weeks for confirmation
options.rationale: ADIPS in pregnancy does not use an intermediate IGT category; any single value above the threshold establishes GDM. Repeating the test delays nutritional and glycaemic management. (Source: ADIPS Consensus Guidelines for GDM)

options.text: Pre-existing type 2 diabetes; commence insulin and refer to endocrinology
options.rationale: Pre-existing diabetes is diagnosed by fasting at least 7.0 mmol/L, random at least 11.1 mmol/L, or HbA1c at least 6.5 percent, none of which apply. Labelling as type 2 would misdirect long-term follow-up. (Source: RACGP Diabetes Handbook)

options.text: Gestational diabetes; commence dietary advice, monitoring, and diabetes referral
options.rationale: ADIPS-IADPSG diagnostic thresholds on 75 g OGTT are fasting at least 5.1 mmol/L, 1-h at least 10.0, or 2-h at least 8.5; a single value above threshold qualifies. The fasting at 5.3 mmol/L establishes GDM. (Source: ADIPS Consensus Guidelines for GDM)

options.text: Borderline result; lifestyle advice only with no formal diabetes referral
options.rationale: ADIPS does not recognise a borderline category in pregnancy and labelling the diagnosis as borderline undertreats the foetal and maternal risk. (Source: ADIPS Consensus Guidelines for GDM)

explanation.summary: The ADIPS-IADPSG diagnostic criteria for GDM on the 75 g OGTT at 24 to 28 weeks gestation are: fasting at least 5.1 mmol/L, 1-h at least 10.0 mmol/L, or 2-h at least 8.5 mmol/L. A single value at or above any of these thresholds establishes GDM. Management begins with dietary modification (carbohydrate distribution, plate model) and home blood glucose monitoring (fasting and 2-h post-prandial), with pharmacotherapy (metformin or insulin) added if targets are not met within 1 to 2 weeks.
key_points: ADIPS-IADPSG GDM cut-offs: fasting >=5.1, 1-h >=10.0, 2-h >=8.5 mmol/L
Any single value at or above threshold diagnoses GDM
No intermediate IGT or borderline category in pregnancy
First-line management: diet + monitoring; pharmacotherapy if targets unmet
explanation.pearls: ADIPS-IADPSG 75 g OGTT at 24 to 28 weeks: fasting 5.1, 1-h 10.0, 2-h 8.5 mmol/L - a single value at threshold diagnoses GDM.

subtopic_detail: Anti-D prophylaxis indications under 20 weeks (NBA)
stem:
A 28-year-old woman, blood group O Rhesus-negative, presents to the early pregnancy assessment unit at 11+3 weeks gestation with one day of light per-vaginal bleeding and mild lower abdominal cramping. Her previous pregnancy was uncomplicated and resulted in a vaginal birth two years ago, after which she received post-natal anti-D 625 IU.

On examination she is haemodynamically stable. Speculum reveals a closed cervical os with a small amount of dark blood in the vault. Bimanual examination is unremarkable. Transvaginal ultrasound demonstrates a singleton intrauterine pregnancy with a foetal pole and cardiac activity at a gestational age of 11+4 weeks. Her partner is known to be Rhesus-positive. Indirect Coombs test confirms she has no detectable anti-D antibodies.

data_table.Vital signs: within normal limits
data_table.Examination: speculum closed os with small dark blood; bimanual unremarkable; abdomen non-tender
data_table.Investigations: TVS singleton IUP with cardiac activity at 11+4 wk; indirect Coombs negative; blood group O Rh-negative

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immunoglobulin management?

options.text: Anti-D immunoglobulin 625 IU intramuscularly within 72 hours of bleeding
options.rationale: The 625 IU dose is the standard antepartum and postpartum dose used at or beyond 20 weeks. Under 12 weeks the recommended dose is the lower 250 IU per NBA guidance. (Source: NBA: Guidelines on the use of Rh(D) Immunoglobulin)
source_refs: NBA: Guidelines on the use of Rh(D) Immunoglobulin

options.text: Anti-D immunoglobulin 1500 IU intramuscularly within 72 hours of bleeding
options.rationale: 1500 IU is reserved for large feto-maternal haemorrhage (over 6 mL foetal blood) such as that detected by Kleihauer-Betke testing after major events, not first-trimester threatened miscarriage. (Source: NBA: Guidelines on the use of Rh(D) Immunoglobulin)
source_refs: NBA: Guidelines on the use of Rh(D) Immunoglobulin

options.text: No anti-D required for threatened miscarriage with closed os under 12 weeks
options.rationale: NBA Australia does recommend anti-D 250 IU for any sensitising event in a Rhesus-negative woman under 12 weeks, including threatened miscarriage with bleeding, regardless of cervical os status. (Source: NBA: Guidelines on the use of Rh(D) Immunoglobulin)
source_refs: NBA: Guidelines on the use of Rh(D) Immunoglobulin

options.text: Defer anti-D and arrange Kleihauer-Betke test to quantify foeto-maternal haemorrhage
options.rationale: Kleihauer-Betke testing is unreliable and not recommended before 20 weeks because foetal red cell volume is too small to quantify. Anti-D is given empirically by gestational-age band. (Source: NBA: Guidelines on the use of Rh(D) Immunoglobulin)
source_refs: NBA: Guidelines on the use of Rh(D) Immunoglobulin

options.text: Anti-D immunoglobulin 250 IU intramuscularly within 72 hours of bleeding
options.rationale: NBA recommends anti-D 250 IU for sensitising events under 12 weeks gestation in a Rhesus-negative woman, including threatened miscarriage with bleeding. Timing within 72 hours is critical to prevent sensitisation. (Source: NBA: Guidelines on the use of Rh(D) Immunoglobulin)
source_refs: NBA: Guidelines on the use of Rh(D) Immunoglobulin

explanation.summary: NBA anti-D dosing in Australia is banded by gestational age. Under 12 weeks the dose for any sensitising event (including bleeding in threatened, complete, or incomplete miscarriage) is 250 IU within 72 hours. From 12 weeks onwards the dose is 625 IU. Routine antenatal prophylaxis is given at 28 and 34 weeks (625 IU each). Postpartum dose is 625 IU plus dose adjustment by Kleihauer if FMH exceeds 6 mL foetal blood. Kleihauer testing is not used before 20 weeks because of poor reliability.
key_points: Anti-D under 12 weeks: 250 IU within 72 hours of sensitising event
Anti-D 12 weeks and over: 625 IU within 72 hours
Routine antenatal prophylaxis at 28 and 34 weeks
Kleihauer testing not used before 20 weeks
explanation.pearls: In a Rhesus-negative woman with bleeding under 12 weeks, give anti-D 250 IU IM within 72 hours; the dose doubles to 625 IU from 12 weeks onward.

sources.label: NBA: Guidelines on the use of Rh(D) Immunoglobulin

subtopic_detail: Mode of delivery for DCDA twins at 37 weeks
stem:
A 31-year-old primigravida with a spontaneous dichorionic diamniotic twin pregnancy attends a 36-week obstetric review. Both foetuses are alive and growing concordantly, with estimated foetal weights at the 50th and 55th centiles and an inter-twin discordance of 6 percent. She has no medical co-morbidities and the pregnancy has been uncomplicated.

On examination her symphysis-fundal height is 42 cm. Twin 1 is cephalic with engaged presenting part; twin 2 is transverse with the foetal back inferior. Cervical examination reveals a closed, posterior cervix. Both placentas are fundal-anterior on the most recent ultrasound, with normal umbilical artery Doppler. She is keen to plan a vaginal birth at 37+5 weeks gestation, the unit's standard timing for DCDA twins.

data_table.Vital signs: within normal limits
data_table.Examination: SFH 42 cm; twin 1 cephalic engaged; twin 2 transverse; cervix closed posterior
data_table.Investigations: 36-wk ultrasound: DCDA twins, EFW 50th and 55th centile (6% discordance), fundal-anterior placentas, normal umbilical Doppler

lead_in: What is the most appropriate counselling regarding mode of birth?

options.text: Recommend elective caesarean at 37+5 weeks because twin 2 is non-cephalic
options.rationale: RANZCOG and the AAOM joint statement permit planned vaginal birth for DCDA twins only if twin 1 is cephalic AND twin 2 is cephalic or amenable to internal podalic version after delivery of twin 1. A transverse second twin is generally an indication for caesarean unless an experienced obstetrician is available for breech extraction or external version. (Source: RANZCOG: Management of twin pregnancy)

options.text: Plan vaginal birth at 37+5 weeks with internal podalic version of twin 2 in labour
options.rationale: Internal podalic version is acceptable only when performed by an obstetrician experienced in the manoeuvre. Listing it without confirmed clinician competency exposes mother and twin 2 to avoidable risk. (Source: RANZCOG: Management of twin pregnancy)

options.text: Plan vaginal birth at 38+5 weeks regardless of twin 2 presentation in labour
options.rationale: Continuing the DCDA pregnancy beyond 37+6 increases the risk of stillbirth, and the proposed plan ignores the non-cephalic second twin entirely. (Source: RANZCOG: Management of twin pregnancy)

options.text: Defer delivery decision until spontaneous labour and manage twin 2 in labour
options.rationale: RANZCOG advises elective birth of DCDA twins by 37+6 weeks. Waiting for spontaneous labour delays a planned decision and leaves the unit reacting to a transverse second twin at presentation. (Source: RANZCOG: Management of twin pregnancy)

options.text: Recommend elective caesarean at 36+0 weeks due to twin pregnancy risk profile
options.rationale: DCDA twins are delivered at 37+0 to 37+6 weeks unless complications dictate earlier. Bringing the delivery forward to 36+0 without indication adds avoidable neonatal respiratory morbidity. (Source: RANZCOG: Management of twin pregnancy)

explanation.summary: RANZCOG and AAOM recommend planned birth of uncomplicated DCDA twins at 37+0 to 37+6 weeks. Mode of birth depends on the presentation of twin 1 and the unit's expertise. Planned vaginal birth is appropriate when twin 1 is cephalic AND twin 2 is cephalic, breech in a unit experienced in vaginal breech, or transverse with a clinician confident in internal podalic version. A transverse second twin without that expertise is an indication for elective caesarean. MCMA twins always deliver by caesarean at 32 to 34 weeks; MCDA at 36 to 37 weeks.
key_points: DCDA twins delivered electively at 37+0 to 37+6 weeks
Planned vaginal birth requires cephalic twin 1; twin 2 presentation determines further pathway
Transverse twin 2 without experienced clinician for podalic version: caesarean
MCMA twins by caesarean at 32 to 34 weeks; MCDA at 36 to 37
explanation.pearls: DCDA twins with non-cephalic twin 2 and no clinician confident in internal podalic version: elective caesarean at 37+5 weeks, not vaginal.

subtopic_detail: Timing of caesarean for asymptomatic placenta praevia
stem:
A 35-year-old multigravida (G3 P2) attends antenatal clinic at 34+5 weeks gestation. A transabdominal scan at 32 weeks identified placenta praevia with the placental edge covering the internal cervical os by 18 mm. A repeat transvaginal scan at 34 weeks confirmed a major praevia with the lower edge 12 mm across the os. She has had no per-vaginal bleeding throughout the pregnancy.

On examination she is well, vital signs within normal limits, with a soft non-tender abdomen. The foetus is in transverse lie with an estimated foetal weight at the 50th centile. Her previous two deliveries were uncomplicated vaginal births. The pregnancy is otherwise low-risk and she has had antenatal corticosteroids in a prior preterm threatened labour at 31 weeks (foetus born at term).

data_table.Vital signs: within normal limits
data_table.Examination: soft non-tender abdomen, foetus transverse, no per-vaginal bleeding
data_table.Investigations: TVS at 34 weeks: placenta covering internal os by 12 mm (major praevia), EFW 50th centile, normal umbilical Doppler

lead_in: What is the most appropriate elective delivery plan?

options.text: Schedule elective lower-segment caesarean at 36+0 to 37+0 weeks gestation
options.rationale: RANZCOG recommends elective caesarean for uncomplicated major placenta praevia at 36+0 to 37+0 weeks to balance neonatal respiratory morbidity against the rising risk of unplanned bleeding at term. (Source: RANZCOG: Placenta praevia and placenta accreta)
source_refs: RANZCOG: Placenta praevia and placenta accreta

options.text: Schedule elective lower-segment caesarean at 39+0 weeks gestation
options.rationale: Waiting until 39 weeks for major praevia accepts a meaningful interval risk of unscheduled antepartum haemorrhage requiring emergent surgery. RANZCOG specifically pulls the planned date earlier. (Source: RANZCOG: Placenta praevia and placenta accreta)
source_refs: RANZCOG: Placenta praevia and placenta accreta

options.text: Schedule elective lower-segment caesarean at 34+0 weeks gestation
options.rationale: 34 weeks delivery for uncomplicated major praevia is too early and incurs avoidable neonatal respiratory morbidity. Earlier delivery is justified only if symptomatic with bleeding. (Source: RANZCOG: Placenta praevia and placenta accreta)
source_refs: RANZCOG: Placenta praevia and placenta accreta

options.text: Plan trial of labour with continuous CTG and an experienced obstetrician on standby
options.rationale: Major praevia is an absolute contraindication to vaginal birth. Any cervical change risks catastrophic haemorrhage as the placenta separates from the lower segment. (Source: RANZCOG: Placenta praevia and placenta accreta)
source_refs: RANZCOG: Placenta praevia and placenta accreta

options.text: Repeat ultrasound at 37 weeks before deciding on delivery timing
options.rationale: Repeat scanning will not move a placenta covering the os by 12 mm; the decision to schedule caesarean before 37 weeks is already established. Further imaging adds delay without changing the plan. (Source: RANZCOG: Placenta praevia and placenta accreta)
source_refs: RANZCOG: Placenta praevia and placenta accreta

explanation.summary: For uncomplicated major placenta praevia (placental edge covering the internal os by more than 20 mm, or persistent praevia after 32 weeks), RANZCOG recommends elective lower-segment caesarean at 36+0 to 37+0 weeks. Earlier delivery is indicated if there has been antepartum haemorrhage. The lower segment caesarean must be performed by an experienced obstetrician with consultant anaesthesia, cross-matched blood available, and consideration of placenta accreta spectrum imaging if there are risk factors (prior caesarean, IVF). Major praevia is an absolute contraindication to vaginal birth.
key_points: Uncomplicated major praevia: elective LSCS 36+0 to 37+0 weeks (RANZCOG)
Symptomatic praevia (bleeding): earlier delivery individualised by gestation and bleeding
Major praevia absolutely contraindicates vaginal birth
Screen for accreta spectrum if prior caesarean or IVF
explanation.pearls: Asymptomatic major placenta praevia: elective LSCS at 36 to 37 weeks, with cross-matched blood and consultant cover; earlier delivery only for bleeding.

sources.label: RANZCOG: Placenta praevia and placenta accreta

subtopic_detail: Management of PPROM at 34 to 36 weeks (RANZCOG)
stem:
A 27-year-old primigravida at 34+5 weeks gestation presents with a 4-hour history of clear fluid loss per vaginam. She felt a small gush followed by ongoing dampness. There is no abdominal pain, no per-vaginal bleeding, and the foetus is moving normally.

On examination she is afebrile with pulse 78, blood pressure 118 by 72 mmHg, respiratory rate 16, SpO2 99 percent on room air, temperature 36.8 degrees Celsius. Speculum reveals a pool of clear fluid in the posterior fornix that ferns on slide testing. The cervix is closed. CTG shows a baseline of 140 with normal variability and accelerations. White cell count 9.2 x10^9/L (4-11) and CRP 4 mg/L (<5). Group B streptococcus screen at 28 weeks was negative.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 78, BP 118/72, RR 16, SpO2 99% room air, temperature 36.8 degrees Celsius
data_table.Examination: speculum: clear fluid pool, ferning positive, cervix closed; abdomen soft non-tender
data_table.Investigations: WCC 9.2 x10^9/L (4-11), CRP 4 mg/L (<5), GBS negative at 28 wk, CTG baseline 140 normal variability with accelerations

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management plan?

options.text: Admit, commence IV benzylpenicillin and erythromycin and induce labour with oxytocin now
options.rationale: Active induction at 34+5 weeks is not the default RANZCOG position because the PPROMT trial showed worse neonatal outcomes with immediate delivery between 34+0 and 36+6 compared with expectant management in well-monitored women. (Source: RANZCOG: PPROM; PPROMT Trial 2016)
source_refs: RANZCOG: Preterm prelabour rupture of membranes

options.text: Admit, latency erythromycin and expectant management with daily CTG and inflammatory markers
options.rationale: RANZCOG recommends expectant management for PPROM between 34+0 and 36+6 weeks in the absence of chorioamnionitis, foetal compromise, or placental abruption, following PPROMT trial evidence of reduced neonatal respiratory distress with expectant care. Latency antibiotics reduce ascending infection. (Source: RANZCOG: PPROM)
source_refs: RANZCOG: Preterm prelabour rupture of membranes

options.text: Discharge home on oral amoxicillin with daily clinic review and self-temperature monitoring
options.rationale: PPROM management requires inpatient surveillance with CTG and inflammatory markers; discharge home cannot reliably detect early chorioamnionitis. (Source: RANZCOG: PPROM)
source_refs: RANZCOG: Preterm prelabour rupture of membranes

options.text: Administer betamethasone 11.4 mg IM and proceed to elective caesarean at 35+0 weeks
options.rationale: Beyond 34+0 weeks the benefit of antenatal corticosteroids is uncertain and routine use is not recommended in PPROM at this gestation. Proceeding to caesarean adds surgical morbidity without clear benefit. (Source: RANZCOG: Antenatal corticosteroids)

options.text: Administer tocolysis with nifedipine and continue surveillance until 37 weeks gestation
options.rationale: Tocolysis is not indicated in PPROM because membranes are already ruptured and prolonging gestation in the presence of broken membranes risks ascending infection without clear benefit. (Source: RANZCOG: PPROM)
source_refs: RANZCOG: Preterm prelabour rupture of membranes

explanation.summary: RANZCOG management of PPROM is gestation-banded. Under 34+0 weeks, expectant care with antenatal corticosteroids, magnesium sulfate (under 30 weeks), and erythromycin latency antibiotics is standard. Between 34+0 and 36+6 weeks, the PPROMT trial demonstrated lower neonatal respiratory morbidity with expectant management compared with immediate delivery; expectant care with daily monitoring is preferred unless there is chorioamnionitis, foetal compromise, abruption, or active labour. From 37+0 weeks, induction is reasonable.
key_points: PPROM 34+0 to 36+6 weeks: expectant management is RANZCOG default (post-PPROMT)
Latency antibiotics (erythromycin) given until labour or delivery
Daily CTG and inflammatory markers to detect chorioamnionitis early
Triggers for immediate delivery: chorioamnionitis, abruption, foetal compromise, labour
explanation.pearls: PPROM between 34+0 and 36+6 weeks without chorioamnionitis or foetal compromise: expectant management with latency erythromycin (PPROMT trial 2016).

sources.label: RANZCOG: Preterm prelabour rupture of membranes

subtopic_detail: Ectopic in only remaining tube after prior contralateral salpingectomy
stem:
A 33-year-old woman G2 P0 presents to the early pregnancy assessment service with three days of right iliac fossa pain and a positive home pregnancy test. Her last menstrual period was 7 weeks ago. Three years ago she had an emergency laparoscopic left salpingectomy for a ruptured ectopic pregnancy; she has no other gynaecological surgery and no children. She would like to preserve every reasonable chance of future spontaneous conception.

On examination she is haemodynamically stable. Abdomen is soft with mild right iliac fossa tenderness without peritonism. Transvaginal ultrasound shows an empty uterus with a 3 cm right adnexal mass containing a yolk sac but no foetal cardiac activity, with a small amount of free fluid in the pouch of Douglas. Serum bhCG is 2400 IU/L. FBC, group and hold, and electrolytes are within normal limits. The left ovary is structurally normal and the left tube is absent on imaging.

data_table.Vital signs: within normal limits
data_table.Examination: abdomen soft with mild RIF tenderness, no peritonism, no cervical excitation
data_table.Investigations: TVS: empty uterus, 3 cm right adnexal mass with yolk sac, no cardiac activity, small free fluid in pouch of Douglas; bhCG 2400 IU/L; left tube absent (prior salpingectomy)

lead_in: What is the most appropriate surgical procedure if she opts for operative management?

options.text: Laparoscopic right salpingostomy with conservative tube preservation
options.rationale: Salpingostomy preserves the affected tube but carries a 4 to 15 percent risk of persistent trophoblast requiring further treatment, plus higher risk of recurrent ipsilateral ectopic. In a single-tube setting where future ectopic in the same tube would be catastrophic, salpingectomy gives better long-term outcomes. (Source: RANZCOG GPS: Tubal ectopic pregnancy)

options.text: Methotrexate 50 mg/m2 IM single-dose protocol with weekly bhCG follow-up
options.rationale: Methotrexate is medical not surgical management, and the question asks about the surgical procedure. Methotrexate is also a legitimate alternative here given bhCG below 5000 and stable haemodynamics, but is outside the lead-in. (Source: RANZCOG GPS: Tubal ectopic pregnancy)

options.text: Laparoscopic ipsilateral oophorectomy with tubal preservation
options.rationale: The ovary is not implicated in tubal ectopic pregnancy; removing it would compromise fertility (through reduced ovarian reserve and contralateral compensation issues) without addressing the ectopic. (Source: RANZCOG GPS: Tubal ectopic pregnancy)

options.text: Laparoscopic right salpingectomy with histopathology of the resected tube
options.rationale: Despite this being her remaining tube, the historical-bilaterality flip applies: with no contralateral tube, recurrent ipsilateral ectopic risk dominates fertility planning, and salpingectomy with subsequent IVF gives better cumulative live-birth outcomes than salpingostomy with the risks of persistent trophoblast and recurrence. RANZCOG endorses salpingectomy in this scenario. (Source: RANZCOG GPS: Tubal ectopic pregnancy)

options.text: Laparotomy with right salpingectomy and contralateral tube preservation review
options.rationale: Laparotomy is reserved for haemodynamic instability or limited laparoscopic access. The patient is stable and laparoscopy is preferred, with shorter recovery and equivalent outcomes. (Source: RANZCOG GPS: Tubal ectopic pregnancy)

explanation.summary: Contrary to the intuitive 'preserve the only remaining tube' approach, RANZCOG and the recent meta-analytic evidence favour laparoscopic salpingectomy over salpingostomy when an ectopic occurs in the sole remaining tube after prior contralateral salpingectomy. Salpingostomy carries 4 to 15 percent persistent trophoblast risk and substantially higher recurrent ipsilateral ectopic risk; subsequent ectopic in that single tube would necessitate emergency salpingectomy under worse conditions. Cumulative live-birth rates via salpingectomy plus planned IVF exceed those of salpingostomy attempting natural conception. Medical management with methotrexate is an acceptable alternative given bhCG below 5000 and stable haemodynamics, but if operative is chosen, salpingectomy is preferred.
key_points: Historical-bilaterality flip: prior contralateral salpingectomy changes the calculus
Salpingostomy in sole remaining tube: high persistent trophoblast and recurrence risk
Salpingectomy + planned IVF gives better cumulative live-birth than salpingostomy
Methotrexate (medical) is a legitimate alternative under RANZCOG criteria (bhCG <5000, no cardiac activity, no rupture)
explanation.pearls: Ectopic in the only remaining tube after prior contralateral salpingectomy: prefer salpingectomy plus planned IVF over salpingostomy because the recurrence and persistent trophoblast risks dominate.

sources.label: Cochrane: Interventions for tubal ectopic pregnancy

subtopic_detail: Long-term lithium monitoring schedule
stem:
A 38-year-old woman with bipolar I disorder has been stable on lithium 750 mg at night for the past 18 months following hospitalisation for a manic episode. She is reviewed in the community mental health clinic.

She has had no mood episodes, no medication side effects, and has been working full time. Her last serum lithium level was 0.7 mmol/L (target 0.6 to 0.8 for maintenance) six months ago. Her last full set of monitoring bloods was at the time of initiation 18 months ago, which she completed without prompting. She brings printed copies of those baseline results, all of which were within normal limits including TSH, calcium, creatinine, and full blood count.

data_table.Vital signs: within normal limits
data_table.Examination: no fine tremor, no goitre, euthymic mood, no peripheral oedema
data_table.Investigations: last lithium level 0.7 mmol/L six months ago; no monitoring bloods done since initiation 18 months ago

lead_in: What is the most appropriate ongoing monitoring schedule?

options.text: Lithium level annually with TSH, creatinine and calcium only if symptomatic
options.rationale: Annual lithium and selective biochemistry under-monitors a drug with narrow therapeutic index and predictable renal and thyroid effects, and would miss subclinical change before symptoms develop. (Source: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines for Mood Disorders)
source_refs: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines for Mood Disorders

options.text: Lithium level monthly and TSH plus creatinine 3-monthly indefinitely
options.rationale: Monthly lithium levels are appropriate during titration and the first 6 months; ongoing maintenance does not require this frequency in a stable patient and over-investigates without clinical benefit. (Source: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines for Mood Disorders)
source_refs: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines for Mood Disorders

options.text: Lithium level only when dose changes or symptoms suggest toxicity or relapse
options.rationale: Reactive-only monitoring misses asymptomatic level drift from dehydration, NSAIDs, ACE inhibitors, and dose-adherence change. RANZCP requires routine surveillance even in stable patients. (Source: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines for Mood Disorders)
source_refs: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines for Mood Disorders

options.text: Lithium level 6-monthly with full blood count alone every 12 months
options.rationale: FBC alone misses the key targets of TSH (lithium causes hypothyroidism in 5 to 35 percent over time) and creatinine (cumulative renal injury), and would leave thyroid and renal change undetected. (Source: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines for Mood Disorders)
source_refs: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines for Mood Disorders

options.text: Lithium level 6-monthly; TSH and creatinine 6-monthly; calcium and FBC 12-monthly
options.rationale: RANZCP guidelines for stable maintenance lithium specify level every 6 months, TSH and creatinine every 6 months, and calcium with FBC annually to detect hypothyroidism, renal decline, and hyperparathyroidism. (Source: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines for Mood Disorders)
source_refs: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines for Mood Disorders

explanation.summary: Long-term lithium monitoring targets three predictable adverse effects: thyroid dysfunction (5 to 35 percent develop hypothyroidism over years), renal impairment (cumulative tubular and interstitial injury), and primary hyperparathyroidism (raised calcium with inappropriately normal or elevated PTH). RANZCP recommends serum lithium every 6 months once stable (more often if dose changed, dehydration, new interacting medication), TSH and creatinine every 6 months, and calcium with FBC annually. ACE inhibitors, ARBs, NSAIDs, thiazides, and dehydration all raise lithium levels and prompt earlier rechecking.
key_points: Stable maintenance: lithium level + TSH + creatinine 6-monthly
Calcium and FBC annually (detect hyperparathyroidism, marrow effects)
Recheck level promptly if dehydration, NSAIDs, ACEi/ARB, or thiazide added
Lithium hypothyroidism affects 5 to 35 percent of long-term users
explanation.pearls: Lithium maintenance monitoring: level + TSH + creatinine every 6 months and calcium annually to detect hypothyroidism, renal decline, and hyperparathyroidism.

sources.label: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines for Mood Disorders

subtopic_detail: Antipsychotic metabolic monitoring schedule
stem:
A 24-year-old man with first-episode psychosis was commenced on olanzapine 10 mg nightly two months ago after presenting to the early psychosis intervention service. He has responded well: persecutory delusions have resolved and he is sleeping consistently. He has no past medical history, does not smoke, and drinks alcohol once or twice a month.

Baseline assessment at the time of starting olanzapine included weight 78 kg, BMI 24, BP 122 by 78 mmHg, fasting glucose 4.8 mmol/L (3.0-5.4), total cholesterol 4.2 mmol/L (less than 5.5), LDL 2.4 mmol/L, HDL 1.3 mmol/L, triglycerides 1.0 mmol/L, and a normal ECG. He attends a routine community follow-up appointment.

data_table.Vital signs: within normal limits
data_table.Examination: no extrapyramidal signs, no postural drop
data_table.Investigations: baseline weight 78 kg BMI 24, fasting glucose 4.8 mmol/L (3.0-5.4), cholesterol 4.2 mmol/L, LDL 2.4, HDL 1.3, triglycerides 1.0, ECG normal

lead_in: When is the next set of antipsychotic-related metabolic monitoring bloods due?

options.text: At 12 weeks from initiation (around 4 weeks from now), then annually if stable
options.rationale: The RANZCP and Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic schedule for second-generation antipsychotic monitoring is baseline, then 12 weeks, then annually for fasting glucose and lipids, with weight at every visit and BP at every visit. (Source: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines; Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)
source_refs: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines for Schizophrenia

options.text: At 6 weeks from initiation, then 12 weeks, then annually if stable on therapy
options.rationale: A 6-week check is not part of the RANZCP or AMH schedule for routine metabolic monitoring; the first repeat is at 12 weeks. Adding a 6-week point does not change management. (Source: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines)
source_refs: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines for Schizophrenia

options.text: At 6 months from initiation, then annually if metabolic measures remain stable
options.rationale: A 6-month first repeat misses the early window for olanzapine-related weight gain and dyslipidaemia, where intervention (switch agent or commence metformin) is most effective. (Source: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines)
source_refs: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines for Schizophrenia

options.text: At 12 months from initiation if asymptomatic, then yearly thereafter on therapy
options.rationale: Annual monitoring alone misses early metabolic side effects that emerge within the first 3 months on olanzapine in particular. RANZCP specifies the 12-week timepoint. (Source: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines)
source_refs: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines for Schizophrenia

options.text: Only if weight gain, hypertension, or new symptoms develop on therapy
options.rationale: Reactive-only monitoring misses asymptomatic dyslipidaemia and impaired fasting glucose, which are the targets of antipsychotic metabolic screening. (Source: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines)
source_refs: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines for Schizophrenia

explanation.summary: Second-generation antipsychotics (especially olanzapine and clozapine) cause weight gain, dyslipidaemia, and impaired glucose tolerance early in treatment. Australian monitoring per RANZCP and Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic is structured to detect this window. Baseline assessment includes weight, BMI, waist circumference, BP, fasting glucose, fasting lipids, and ECG. Repeat fasting glucose and lipids at 12 weeks (the first checkpoint), then annually thereafter if stable. Weight and BP are checked at every visit. Prolactin is checked if symptomatic.
key_points: Antipsychotic metabolic monitoring schedule: baseline, 12 weeks, then annually
Weight and BP every visit; fasting glucose and lipids per schedule
Olanzapine and clozapine carry the highest metabolic burden
Switch agent or add metformin if early dyslipidaemia or weight gain
explanation.pearls: Antipsychotic metabolic screen: baseline, 12 weeks, then yearly for fasting glucose and lipids; weight and BP every visit.

sources.label: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines for Schizophrenia

subtopic_detail: Pharmacological management of acute behavioural disturbance
stem:
A 28-year-old man is brought to the emergency department by paramedics under a section of the Mental Health Act after threatening neighbours with a baseball bat. He has a documented history of schizophrenia and methamphetamine use. He has not taken any prescribed medications for the past two weeks.

In the resuscitation cubicle he is shouting, pacing, and intermittently striking the walls. Verbal de-escalation by experienced mental health nurses for 20 minutes has failed. Security staff are present. Pulse is 110, blood pressure 138 by 84 mmHg, respiratory rate 22, SpO2 99 percent on room air, temperature 37.0 degrees Celsius. He weighs approximately 80 kg. He is now physically restrained. There is no history of cardiac disease, no QT-prolonging medications, and no opioid use.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 110, BP 138/84, RR 22, SpO2 99% room air, temperature 37.0 degrees Celsius
data_table.Examination: agitated, mydriasis, no diaphoresis, no clonus, no rigidity
data_table.Investigations: weight approximately 80 kg; no ECG yet; no recent QT-prolonging medications

lead_in: What is the most appropriate first-line pharmacological intervention?

options.text: Diazepam 10 mg orally with continued verbal de-escalation effort
options.rationale: Oral diazepam is unlikely to be safely administered to an actively combative patient under physical restraint, and onset is too slow for the immediate safety risk. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic - Acute behavioural disturbance)
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic - Acute behavioural disturbance

options.text: Intramuscular olanzapine 10 mg with separated benzodiazepine if needed later
options.rationale: Therapeutic Guidelines recommend IM olanzapine 10 mg as first-line for severe acute behavioural disturbance without contraindication, with strict avoidance of co-administration with parenteral benzodiazepines due to respiratory depression risk. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic - Acute behavioural disturbance)
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic - Acute behavioural disturbance

options.text: Combined IM haloperidol 10 mg plus IM lorazepam 2 mg given simultaneously now
options.rationale: Co-administration of IM olanzapine or IM haloperidol with parenteral benzodiazepines is associated with respiratory depression and hypotension; this combination is no longer recommended as first-line in Australia. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic - Acute behavioural disturbance

options.text: Oral quetiapine 300 mg with continued seclusion until adequate sedation occurs
options.rationale: Oral quetiapine in this dose has slow onset and is impractical to administer to an actively restrained agitated patient; parenteral therapy is needed for immediate safety. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic - Acute behavioural disturbance)
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic - Acute behavioural disturbance

options.text: Continue physical restraint and seclusion only without pharmacological intervention
options.rationale: Prolonged physical restraint without pharmacological calming is associated with rhabdomyolysis, asphyxia, and increased death rate; chemical restraint is part of the standard of care once verbal de-escalation has failed. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic - Acute behavioural disturbance

explanation.summary: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic recommends IM olanzapine 10 mg (5 mg if elderly or frail) as first-line monotherapy for severe acute behavioural disturbance unresponsive to verbal de-escalation. IM droperidol 10 mg is an acceptable alternative (with QT assessment if possible). Combined parenteral antipsychotic plus benzodiazepine carries a respiratory-depression risk and should be separated by at least 1 hour. Continue monitoring including pulse oximetry, BP, and sedation level (RASS) after administration. Physical restraint alone is associated with worse outcomes than appropriate chemical calming.
key_points: First-line: IM olanzapine 10 mg (or IM droperidol 10 mg) monotherapy
Avoid simultaneous parenteral antipsychotic plus parenteral benzodiazepine
Reduce dose in elderly, frail, or naive patients
Continue vital sign and sedation-score monitoring after dose
explanation.pearls: Acute behavioural disturbance first-line in Australia is IM olanzapine 10 mg; never co-administer parenterally with a benzodiazepine because of respiratory depression risk.

sources.label: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic - Acute behavioural disturbance

subtopic_detail: Tricyclic antidepressant overdose with QRS prolongation
stem:
A 42-year-old woman with major depressive disorder is brought to the emergency department by her partner approximately two hours after ingesting an unknown quantity of her amitriptyline 50 mg tablets in a deliberate self-poisoning event. The original 100-tablet bottle is now empty.

On arrival she is drowsy with GCS 11 (E3 V3 M5), pulse 128, blood pressure 92 by 58 mmHg, respiratory rate 14, SpO2 96 percent on room air, temperature 36.5 degrees Celsius. Pupils are 6 mm bilaterally and reactive. She has dry mucous membranes and absent bowel sounds. The 12-lead ECG demonstrates sinus tachycardia with a QRS duration of 132 ms and a terminal R wave of 4 mm in lead aVR. Venous blood gas shows pH 7.30, bicarbonate 19 mmol/L (22-32). Potassium is 3.7 mmol/L (3.5-5.0). Paracetamol level is undetectable.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 128, BP 92/58, RR 14, SpO2 96% room air, temperature 36.5 degrees Celsius
data_table.Examination: GCS 11 (E3V3M5), dry mucous membranes, absent bowel sounds, pupils 6 mm reactive
data_table.Investigations: ECG sinus tachycardia, QRS 132 ms, terminal R wave aVR 4 mm; pH 7.30, bicarbonate 19 mmol/L (22-32), K 3.7 mmol/L (3.5-5.0); paracetamol undetectable

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate pharmacological intervention?

options.text: Activated charcoal 50 g orally with intravenous fluids and observation only
options.rationale: Two hours post-ingestion with depressed conscious state, charcoal has limited benefit and aspiration risk is meaningful. More importantly, the widened QRS and metabolic acidosis demand active sodium-channel-blockade reversal. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology; Murray TCA overdose)

options.text: Intravenous flumazenil 0.2 mg followed by intravenous fluid resuscitation
options.rationale: Flumazenil is contraindicated in mixed overdose including TCA because it can precipitate seizures, and there is no benzodiazepine indication anyway. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology)

options.text: Intravenous sodium bicarbonate 1 to 2 mmol/kg bolus, then titrate to pH 7.5
options.rationale: QRS more than 100 ms after TCA overdose indicates fast-sodium-channel blockade and predicts arrhythmia and seizures. IV sodium bicarbonate (1 to 2 mmol/kg bolus, then infusion or repeat boluses to target arterial pH 7.50 to 7.55) is the antidote and reverses sodium-channel block. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology; Murray TCA overdose)

options.text: Intravenous magnesium sulfate 2 g bolus over 15 minutes for arrhythmia prevention
options.rationale: Magnesium is appropriate for polymorphic VT or torsades but the live problem here is sodium-channel blockade, which responds to sodium bicarbonate not magnesium. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology)

options.text: Intravenous lidocaine infusion at 1 to 4 mg/min for ventricular arrhythmia prevention
options.rationale: Lidocaine is a sodium-channel blocker itself and worsens the TCA toxicity; class IA and IC antiarrhythmics are contraindicated. Sodium bicarbonate is the answer. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology)

explanation.summary: Tricyclic antidepressant overdose causes fast sodium channel blockade, anticholinergic toxidrome, and alpha-1 antagonism. The cardinal ECG indicator is QRS prolongation; thresholds of more than 100 ms predict seizures and more than 160 ms predict ventricular arrhythmias. A terminal R wave in aVR of more than 3 mm is another marker. IV sodium bicarbonate 1 to 2 mmol/kg bolus, repeated to maintain arterial pH 7.50 to 7.55, reverses sodium-channel block. Avoid class I antiarrhythmics. Hypotension is treated with fluids then noradrenaline (alpha-1 agonist counters the alpha-1 blockade). Lipid emulsion is rescue therapy.
key_points: QRS more than 100 ms after TCA overdose predicts seizures and arrhythmia
Terminal R wave aVR more than 3 mm is another marker of toxicity
IV sodium bicarbonate 1 to 2 mmol/kg, repeat to pH 7.50 to 7.55
Avoid class I antiarrhythmics; flumazenil contraindicated in mixed overdose
explanation.pearls: TCA overdose with QRS more than 100 ms: IV sodium bicarbonate 1 to 2 mmol/kg bolus, titrate to arterial pH 7.50 to 7.55; class I antiarrhythmics and flumazenil are contraindicated.

subtopic_detail: Valproate use in women of childbearing potential
stem:
A 27-year-old woman with bipolar I disorder has been stable on sodium valproate 1000 mg daily for two years. Prior to valproate she had three manic episodes requiring hospitalisation, two of which were precipitated by lithium discontinuation due to renal concerns at the time (creatinine has since normalised). Her current valproate level is 500 micromol/L (350-700) and her last manic episode was three years ago.

She attends her psychiatry follow-up to share that she and her partner are planning to conceive within the next 6 to 12 months. She has been using a contraceptive intrauterine device since starting valproate. There is no relevant family history, no medical comorbidity, and she takes no other medications.

data_table.Vital signs: within normal limits
data_table.Examination: euthymic, no extrapyramidal signs, no fine tremor
data_table.Investigations: valproate level 500 micromol/L (350-700); LFTs, FBC, creatinine within normal limits

lead_in: What is the most appropriate medication-management plan?

options.text: Continue valproate with high-dose folic acid 5 mg daily during planning and pregnancy
options.rationale: Folic acid supplementation does not mitigate valproate's spina bifida and developmental risk; valproate-related neurodevelopmental harm and 10 percent major malformation rate persist despite folate. (Source: RANZCP Mood Disorders CPG; TGA Valproate Pregnancy Prevention)
source_refs: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines for Mood Disorders

options.text: Continue valproate until pregnancy confirmed, then cease and switch to lamotrigine
options.rationale: Major teratogenic effects of valproate occur in the early first trimester before pregnancy is often confirmed; stopping at that point is too late to prevent neural-tube defects. (Source: TGA Valproate Pregnancy Prevention Program)

options.text: Reduce valproate to 500 mg daily during planning to minimise teratogenicity risk overall
options.rationale: Although risk is dose-related, no dose of valproate is considered safe in pregnancy and the 500 mg dose is also associated with neurodevelopmental harm; switching the agent is preferred. (Source: TGA Valproate Pregnancy Prevention Program)

options.text: Cross-titrate from valproate to lithium with cardiology echo screen and pre-conception planning
options.rationale: Lithium is the preferred mood stabiliser for bipolar I in women planning pregnancy because of valproate's 10 percent major malformation rate and substantial neurodevelopmental impact. The plan acknowledges Ebstein anomaly risk (approximately 1 in 1000) via foetal echo at 18 to 20 weeks. (Source: COPE Perinatal Mental Health Guideline; RANZCP Mood Disorders CPG)
source_refs: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines for Mood Disorders

options.text: Stop valproate immediately and commence high-dose olanzapine 20 mg nightly indefinitely
options.rationale: Abrupt valproate cessation risks rapid relapse, and olanzapine 20 mg long-term carries substantial metabolic burden; cross-titration with a mood stabiliser is preferred. (Source: RANZCP Mood Disorders CPG)
source_refs: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines for Mood Disorders

explanation.summary: Sodium valproate causes major congenital malformation in around 10 percent of exposed pregnancies (especially neural-tube defects) and neurodevelopmental impairment in around 30 to 40 percent. TGA imposes a Pregnancy Prevention Program (PPP) requiring annual risk acknowledgement and effective contraception in all women of childbearing potential. When pregnancy is planned, switching to a safer mood stabiliser before conception is the standard approach. Lithium is the preferred option for bipolar I with risk of Ebstein anomaly approximately 1 in 1000, foetal echo at 18 to 20 weeks. Lamotrigine is an alternative for bipolar depression-predominant illness. Stopping valproate during pregnancy is too late to prevent first-trimester teratogenicity.
key_points: Valproate has 10 percent major malformation rate and substantial neurodevelopmental risk
Switch agent before conception, not at pregnancy confirmation
